report: Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; right arm extension with clenched fist and continued left arm flexion. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop in the left arm initially, progressing to bilateral involvement with asynchronous movements. Head turns persist to the left. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion with right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric limb involvement characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension; this evolves into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or tonic–clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; right arm remains still. Later, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient appears unresponsive throughout, though at one point picks up paper with left hand during left arm flexion.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by right arm extension and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension. Rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm develop. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; patient remains unresponsive.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient opens laptop and briefly interacts, then closes it. No further signs of seizure activity observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive. Left arm continues to flex rhythmically while the right arm remains extended.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Right arm flexion followed by a brief pause; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent bilateral tonic stiffening and asynchronous clonic jerks predominantly affecting the left side in some episodes; no further evolution observed in others. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended. In one instance, this progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with persistent unresponsiveness; in another, no further evolution was observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension with right arm extension; bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, with rhythmic clonic jerks developing and spreading symmetrically. Patient becomes unresponsive as movements intensify. Oxygen mask applied as seizure continues.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension in some instances; asynchronous shaking or rhythmic clonic jerks develop in the left arm, spreading to the right. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue in multiple episodes, with patient remaining unresponsive throughout. Some events begin with rightward head version and right arm extension, also progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. One episode features blank stare with left arm flexion and right arm extension without head deviation, followed by bilateral rhythmic jerking. All events are accompanied by unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by head version (predominantly leftward, with one instance of rightward) and concurrent arm movement (flexion or extension, primarily on the ipsilateral side), followed by bilateral tonic stiffening that evolves into asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks, progressing to generalized tonic–clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, then progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient becomes unresponsive as the seizure evolves, with arms exhibiting asynchronous shaking in some episodes and feet remaining still in at least one instance.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive as movements intensify and remains unresponsive throughout the episode. In one instance, a person approaches and interacts with the patient.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive. Left arm continues to flex while right arm remains extended.
Blank stare followed by left hand movements over abdomen; no other signs of seizure activity observed.
Lip smacking followed by left hand placement on abdomen; blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; patient remains unresponsive throughout. No other movements observed.

Blank stare followed by head version to the right with right arm extension; subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic movements developed and the patient remained unresponsive throughout. In another episode, blank stare was followed by leftward head version, persistent right arm extension, and subtle flexion of the left arm, with no further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm persist.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and asymmetric arm movement, with left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing signs: rightward head version with right arm extension or leftward head version with left arm flexion, occasionally accompanied by subtle lip smacking and asymmetric posturing (e.g., left arm flexion with right arm extension). In several instances, these features progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events. No further evolution or additional signs were observed in some episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm ensue. Subtle lip smacking is noted, with the left arm remaining flexed while the right arm shows slight extension. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, observed in multiple episodes, sometimes accompanied by brief rhythmic jerks of the left arm or progression to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. In several instances, this evolved into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Additional patterns include left arm extension with right arm flexion, or rightward head version with right arm extension, occasionally with bilateral clonic evolution. One episode featured left hand movements over the chest with brief vocalization but no further seizure activity. Another showed isolated left arm flexion with rhythmic chest rise, and one involved left arm flexion with transient object holding before dropping it. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare observed in all episodes, sometimes accompanied by mouth opening; chest shows scattered food debris in some instances. Head version occurred unilaterally—leftward in some episodes, rightward in others. Limb involvement varied: left arm flexion and right arm extension noted in separate events, with bilateral tonic-clonic movements developing in some cases. No clear automatisms, clonic jerks, or tonic stiffening observed in all episodes. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events, with one instance noting the patient holding a remote steadily despite apparent impairment.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and rhythmic hand clasping; patient adjusts remote control with both hands during one episode. No clear tonic stiffening or clonic movements observed in these instances, and patient remains unresponsive throughout. Additional episodes involve blank stare followed by leftward head version with left arm flexion and ipsilateral posturing (right arm extended), progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic–clonic movements. Patient becomes or remains unresponsive during all motor events.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement, which includes flexion or extension and, in some instances, rhythmic clonic jerks. In one episode, the initial symptoms are accompanied by subtle lip smacking. The events may progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, but in other cases, no further evolution is observed. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. A nurse approaches during one event and removes a phone from the patient’s hand; in another, a woman enters from below the frame.

Blank stare followed by head version, predominantly to the left, and associated with left arm involvement—either flexion or extension. Subsequently, bilateral tonic–clonic activity develops, characterized by tonic stiffening followed by asynchronous clonic jerks that predominate on the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events; no further evolution or additional signs observed in isolated episodes without motor progression.

No observable seizure activity initially; patient remains motionless. Subsequent episodes begin with a blank stare followed by lateralizing features: either leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension while the left arm remains flexed. These are followed by bilateral tonic–clonic activity characterized by tonic stiffening and asynchronous shaking, predominantly involving the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, often accompanied by right arm extension. Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop subsequently, with asynchronous shaking involving both arms and legs in some instances. Episodes may progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements or generalized tonic stiffening. One episode involved forward bending while handling a laptop, followed by sudden dropping of the device, arm extension, head retraction, and rigid posturing requiring physical intervention. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. No further evolution observed in milder episodes, such as reaching toward a cup following initial motor signs.

Patient exhibits a blank stare followed by leftward head version and abnormal motor activity involving the left arm, which includes flexion, extension, or a sequence of both. This is accompanied by subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks that may be unilateral (left arm) or bilateral, and may progress to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. In one instance, there is asymmetric limb involvement with left arm flexion and right arm extension prior to bilateral clonic jerking. A separate observation notes a brief period of tonic stiffening followed by rapid covering of the face with both arms and subsequent immobility under the covers. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial involvement of the left arm, manifesting as either flexion or extension, with subsequent evolution into bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Tonic phase characterized by bilateral stiffening, often with right arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking that may be asynchronous and predominantly affect the left side. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare, often followed by subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion, with variable involvement of the right arm (remaining still, extended, or involved in bilateral hand movements such as face covering or adjusting head covering). Lip smacking observed in one instance without other motor activity. Patient repeatedly covers face with both hands, sometimes intermittently lowering and reapplying them, while remaining unresponsive throughout all episodes. No clonic jerks, tonic stiffening, or clear lateral evolution observed across events. Some episodes show no further motor progression after initial blank stare.

The patient exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension or left arm flexion. This is succeeded by bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks. In some episodes, head turning to the left accompanies the onset. Throughout all observed events, the patient remains unresponsive. Positional adjustments by nursing staff do not alter the clinical course.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm posturing—either leftward head version with left arm extension or flexion (right arm extended in some instances), or rightward head version with right arm extension—preceding the onset of bilateral tonic stiffening or bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Tonic–clonic activity may evolve from initial focal features. Motor manifestations often begin asymmetrically, with asynchronous or clonic shaking predominantly affecting the left side in some episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended in some instances; subsequent evolution into either bilateral tonic stiffening or rhythmic clonic jerks, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity in some cases. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity, including left arm flexion and right arm extension; bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop in some episodes. Patient becomes unresponsive during the events, with head remaining midline in one instance but consistently turning left in others. Tonic-clonic progression is observed in certain segments, while in others, clonic activity follows initial focal features without clear secondary generalization.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with variable additional features: rhythmic hand movements or hand clasp, without spread to other limbs in some instances. In one instance, the seizure evolved to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Rightward head version with right arm extension was observed once, followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended. Subsequently, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, then bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient becomes unresponsive as the seizure progresses, with arms raised during the episode.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm involvement—either leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension and contralateral arm flexion—progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks and evolving into generalized tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the seizure episodes, with arms flailing asynchronously during the clonic phase.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension in some instances; bilateral tonic–clonic movements develop, including rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient becomes unresponsive with continued jerking and vocalization, remaining unresponsive throughout the event.

- Blank stare followed by a subtle smile; no other motor signs observed. Patient remains still and unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; no other movements observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and brief right arm extension while left arm remains still. No further evolution observed. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; later bilateral tonic–clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral rhythmic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; left arm remains flexed while right arm shows slight extension. No further evolution or additional signs observed. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; left arm remains extended while right arm shows slight flexion. No further evolution or additional signs observed. - Blank stare followed by subtle left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with variable evolution: in some instances, no further progression occurred; in others, progression to bilateral tonic stiffening, rhythmic clonic jerks, or bilateral tonic-clonic movements involving both arms and legs was observed. The right arm often remained extended during the initial phase. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking, without significant head version, limb movements, or lateralizing features. No evolution beyond this stage observed. Patient remained unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remained unresponsive throughout. - Subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion and maintained right arm extension, without further evolution in some cases; in others, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remained unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, then progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity with initial tonic stiffening and subsequent asynchronous shaking predominantly affecting the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing motor features: either leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension and persistent left arm flexion. In most instances, this evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic activity with asynchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive at the onset of motor manifestations, remaining so throughout the event, with movements intensifying during the course of the seizure.

Blank stare followed by head version and ipsilateral arm movement—either flexion or extension—then evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episodes, with involvement observed on both left and right sides, including leftward head version with left arm involvement and rightward head version with right arm extension. Brief clonic jerks of the left arm are noted in some instances prior to generalized seizure progression.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version, left arm extension, and left arm flexion with concurrent right arm flexion and extension; patient reaches out with left hand and later exhibits bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining either flexed or extended; in one instance, subtle left arm flexion occurred without further evolution. Rhythmic clonic jerks developed bilaterally in one episode. During one event, the patient reached out with the left hand and later picked up a remote control. Another episode featured blank stare with subtle lip smacking and gradual left arm extension while the right arm remained flexed. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and unilateral motor signs including left arm flexion or extension and rhythmic clonic jerks of the left hand. Right arm shows intermittent subtle clonic jerks, progressing to rhythmic clonic movements and extension in some episodes. Seizure evolves into bilateral clonic activity with intermittent leftward head turning. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, with the right arm remaining extended; bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue, characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. Patient exhibits asynchronous shaking of the arms during some episodes and remains unresponsive throughout all events.

- Blank stare followed by head version (leftward in most instances, rightward in several) and corresponding arm movement: leftward head version associated with left arm flexion or extension; rightward head version associated with right arm extension and left arm remaining flexed. - Subtle head version observed in some episodes without significant limb evolution; mild unilateral arm flexion or extension may occur, with the contralateral arm remaining still or in opposing posture. - Rhythmic hand movements, including tapping or bilateral repetitive motions, occur in association with blank stare and head deviation; these may be directed toward objects (e.g., laptop, plate, napkin) but do not consistently indicate awareness or purposeful interaction. - In multiple segments, blank stare with head version is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic activity: initial unilateral signs progress to asymmetric, asynchronous clonic jerks involving both arms and legs; tonic stiffening often precedes clonic phase. - Bilateral tonic–clonic movements are characterized by unresponsiveness, open eyes during convulsive phases, and lack of clear postictal reorientation within observation window. - One episode features sudden onset of bilateral tonic stiffening with clonic jerks, abrupt standing, bending forward, and continued asynchronous shaking, suggesting possible hyperkinetic or automotor seizure with secondary generalization. - Some events remain focal with no evolution beyond staring, head deviation, and simple manual automatisms (e.g., reaching, tapping, eating), without progression to bilateral convulsive activity. - No further evolution or additional motor signs observed in several isolated episodes, indicating possible non-convulsive or aborted seizures.

Blank stare observed in all episodes, consistently followed by subtle motor manifestations. These include intermittent lip smacking, isolated right arm flexion, leftward head version with concurrent left arm flexion and right arm extension, and rightward head version with mild right arm extension. No significant head version or limb movements occurred in some episodes, and no further evolution of motor signs was observed in any instance. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version, left arm flexion, and right arm extension; subsequent bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout as movements intensify.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—then progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with subsequent asynchronous tonic–clonic movements involving both arms and legs. The patient becomes unresponsive as the seizure evolves and remains so throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and left arm flexion, with right arm extension and leftward head version; patient remains motionless and unresponsive throughout. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, transitioning into rhythmic clonic jerks.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension or sustained flexion, observed in multiple episodes. Some events begin with asymmetric posturing (left arm flexion and right arm extension) without head deviation. Rhythmic clonic jerks, bilateral tonic–clonic movements, or asynchronous shaking develop in many cases, often starting unilaterally (typically left arm) and spreading bilaterally. Head remains midline in some instances. Additional features include rhythmic lip smacking. Seizure progression is consistently associated with unresponsiveness, which may be present from onset or develop as movements intensify. In one episode, no further motor evolution occurs after initial symptoms. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial left arm flexion or extension, with subsequent evolution to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Tonic stiffening begins asymmetrically, often starting on the left side, followed by asynchronous clonic jerks that spread bilaterally. The patient becomes unresponsive at onset and remains so throughout the seizure.
Rightward head version with right arm extension; left arm remains flexed. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue. Patient becomes unresponsive.

Multiple episodes of blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent evolution to bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks in most instances. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events. One episode featured rightward head version with right arm extension preceding bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Two brief episodes involved subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion with contralateral arm extension without further progression; patient remained unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm often remaining extended. In most episodes, rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, sometimes beginning asynchronously in the left arm before spreading. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements occur in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. In a subset of observations, no further evolution or additional motor signs are noted beyond the initial features.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement, either with flexion or extension. In most cases, the right arm remains extended or shows minimal movement. Subsequently, a range of motor evolutions may occur: rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally or remain focal to the left side; bilateral tonic–clonic movements emerge; or no further progression is observed. One event features abrupt sitting up with both arms extended without further activity. The left leg may also be involved, showing extension and rhythmic jerking. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and/or asymmetric motor signs including left arm flexion, right arm extension, or both. In some instances, this is accompanied by subtle lip smacking. The episodes variably evolve to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic-clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Subsequently, either bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks appear.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and abnormal movements involving the left arm, ranging from flexion to extension. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking occurred in one episode. No responsiveness was observed throughout any of the events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with the right arm remaining extended; patient remains unresponsive. The left arm exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks, while the legs show asynchronous shaking. Right leg rhythmic jerking begins, and the left hand intermittently moves. Subsequently, rightward head version occurs with right arm extension while the left arm remains flexed, and rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm extension or flexion, with the right arm remaining extended in several instances. Some episodes evolve to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the left arm unilaterally or both arms bilaterally, while others exhibit asynchronous shaking of the left arm. Automatisms are not observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. In one instance, the left arm returns to a resting position following interaction with a healthcare provider. Episodes vary in progression but consistently feature initial blank stare, lateralizing signs to the left side, and impaired awareness.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion or extension; in some instances, right arm extends while left arm flexes. - Subtle head version (leftward or rightward) may occur, with mild asymmetric posturing: left arm flexion with or without right arm extension. - Following the initial stare and motor signs, evolution includes either bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic stiffening. - In several observations, no further evolution beyond the initial posturing is noted. - Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by head version and focal motor activity, most commonly leftward head version with left arm extension or flexion. In several instances, this evolved to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic-clonic movements. Asynchronous shaking of the left arm was also observed in one episode. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with subsequent evolution in some instances to bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks. In one instance, blank stare was followed by left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Another event featured blank stare with subtle rightward head version, mild right arm extension, and transition to rhythmic clonic jerks. Episodes consistently involved unresponsiveness throughout, with no further evolution or additional signs observed in certain cases.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with variable involvement of the right arm (ranging from extension to slight extension or no movement). In some episodes, left arm extension or only slight flexion is observed. Subtle leftward head version may occur without prominent limb movements. Bilateral tonic–clonic or rhythmic clonic jerks develop in later stages of several episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. Eyes briefly flutter near the end in at least one instance.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity characterized by right arm extension and left arm flexion, with head turning to the left in several instances. Asymmetric posturing evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening and/or rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. In some episodes, initial right arm extension is followed by left arm lifting and extension without further progression. Other observations include continued clonic jerking bilaterally with maintained unresponsiveness throughout all events. Some episodes feature subtle clonic jerks on the background of sustained right arm extension and left arm flexion, with slight leftward head deviation, without further evolution. Patient remains unresponsive during all observed activity.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric motor activity, most commonly leftward head turning with left arm flexion and right arm extension. Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop in multiple episodes, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity in some instances. Right arm extension with rhythmic clonic jerks and slight leftward head turn is also observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare observed in all episodes, variably accompanied by subtle leftward or rightward head version, upward eye deviation, or no obvious ictal progression. Left arm movement noted in some instances, including brief flexion or lifting to the face; right arm extension occurred during one episode. Bilateral hand movements near the ears were observed in one instance, with hands returning to the lap. One episode progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with loss of responsiveness; patient remained unresponsive in other episodes as well. No further evolution or additional signs observed in several segments. Automatisms were absent except for isolated hand-to-face motions. Patient appeared unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by subtle left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Subtle lip smacking observed with left arm remaining flexed and right arm showing slight extension. Additional episode noted with blank stare and subtle right hand movements. No further evolution or other clear seizure signs observed across episodes.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version. Left arm flexes briefly. Patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and bilateral ear manipulation, with both hands brought to the ears, suggesting possible automatisms. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no other clear seizure signs observed.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing features including leftward or rightward head version and ipsilateral arm involvement—manifesting as flexion (left) or extension (right). In most episodes, this initial focal onset evolves with rhythmic clonic jerks involving the contralateral or bilateral upper limbs, progressing in several cases to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Asynchronous shaking predominantly affecting the upper limbs may occur. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with some seizures showing no further evolution beyond the initial focal motor signs.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion, often accompanied by leftward head version and unresponsiveness. Right arm may remain extended or become involved as part of evolving bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Bilateral asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks may occur, sometimes progressing to generalized tonic-clonic activity. Subtle lip smacking may be present without significant motor signs. In some episodes, the patient lies still with eyes closed, left arm sustained in flexion while right arm remains relaxed, without progression to bilateral convulsions. Patient is consistently unresponsive during all events; in one instance, the patient covered the face with both hands during the seizure.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; left hand reaches toward the face and then rests on the remote control. Right arm extension occurs simultaneously with left arm flexion, after which both hands are raised to head level. No further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking. Left arm either flexes while the right remains extended, or gradually extends while the right remains flexed. No further evolution observed in either case.

Blank stare followed by subtle rightward head version and mild right arm extension. Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking without significant head version or limb movements. Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion and persistent right arm extension. No further evolution or additional signs observed in any episode, and the patient remained unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension with left arm flexed, or leftward head version and left arm flexion with right arm extended; bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks subsequently develop, progressing in some instances to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks of the right hand ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. Subsequently, right hand manipulation of an object occurs, followed by a brief pause with hands resting on the abdomen. No other seizure-related movements or signs are observed.

Blank stare, often accompanied by subtle or overt head version (predominantly leftward, occasionally rightward), precedes all observed events. During these episodes, the patient remains unresponsive throughout. Ocular deviation is consistently to the left, except in one instance with rightward version. Motor manifestations include asymmetric limb involvement: left arm flexion frequently occurs, sometimes mild or brief, while the right arm typically remains extended; conversely, right arm flexion with left arm extension is also observed. One episode features bilateral tonic–clonic activity following initial rightward head version and arm extension. Rhythmic clonic jerks occur in association with head turning to the left. Orofacial automatisms, specifically subtle lip smacking, are noted during some events. In one instance, the patient closes the eyes and raises the left hand to the forehead following left arm flexion and lip smacking. No significant head movement or limb activity is observed in isolated episodes, though blank stare and unresponsiveness persist.

- Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains extended; later, bilateral tonic-clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; no significant head version or limb movements observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking with rightward head version and right arm extension while left arm remains flexed; no further evolution noted. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and slight left arm flexion; no further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion, often accompanied by leftward head version and right arm extension. Subtle lip smacking may occur initially, with occasional fencer posture (left arm extended, right arm flexed) developing later. Some events progress to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or asynchronous shaking of the limbs, with patient remaining unresponsive throughout. One episode involved the left hand moving toward the face and both hands covering the face prior to head version and limb shaking. Not all episodes evolve beyond initial motor signs; in one instance, only facial touching without further seizure activity was observed.

Blank stare followed by various motor manifestations including leftward head version with left arm extension, left arm flexion, bilateral tonic–clonic movements, rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms, and subtle lip smacking. In some instances, the patient rubbed their face with the left hand after the initial blank stare. No significant head version or limb movements were observed in episodes involving only lip smacking. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing motor signs including leftward head version and asymmetric limb posturing, predominantly manifesting as left arm flexion and right arm extension. This is followed by evolution into bilateral asynchronous shaking or rhythmic clonic jerks, progressing in some instances to generalized tonic–clonic movements. In certain episodes, motor activity remains focal without progression, such as isolated rhythmic clonic jerks of the left or right hand or persistent unilateral posturing without further spread. One episode shows right arm extension with a semi-purposeful finger-to-nose gesture despite unresponsiveness, without head version or clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive during all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with the right arm either flexed or showing rhythmic jerking in some instances. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, followed by clonic movements that are initially asynchronous and may spread from the left side to involve both sides. Rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs is observed during the clonic phase. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.
Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. No other movements or automatisms observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening and asynchronous clonic jerks that spread from the left side to both arms. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in some episodes, evolving into generalized tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with movements intensifying during the course of each episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm extension or flexion, then progression to bilateral tonic stiffening. This is followed by asynchronous clonic jerks or rhythmic jerking involving both arms and legs, predominantly affecting the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm movement—either extension or flexion, predominantly on the left side but also observed on the right. Subsequent bilateral tonic stiffening or tonic-clonic movements ensue, with asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. The patient becomes unresponsive at onset and remains so throughout the episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; subsequent bilateral tonic stiffening evolves into asynchronous clonic jerks, predominantly affecting the left side. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout the seizure.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm, which spread asymmetrically and then bilaterally in an asynchronous manner. Subsequently, rightward head version with right arm extension occurs, while the left arm remains flexed, leading to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks and progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases.

Blank stare followed by sudden leftward head version and left arm extension or flexion, with progression to bilateral tonic stiffening and subsequent tonic-clonic movements. Rhythmic or asynchronous clonic jerks spread from the left side to involve all limbs. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening or tonic-clonic movements with asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks spreading from the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion or extension, with concurrent right arm extension in some instances. Bilateral tonic stiffening or tonic–clonic movements ensue, accompanied by asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks affecting both arms and legs. Patient's eyes remain open throughout, with head tossing observed during the episode. Patient remains unresponsive from onset.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with concurrent right arm extension and asymmetric posturing (left arm flexion, right arm extension). Bilateral tonic stiffening evolves into asynchronous clonic jerks, spreading from the left side to involve both arms and legs, with rhythmic bilateral clonic movements also observed. Patient exhibits intermittent hand gestures and repetitive automatisms involving the hands. Eyes remain open throughout, and patient remains unresponsive during and after the event. Episodic abrupt sitting up may occur during the course of the event.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, progressing to either: (1) left arm flexion with right arm extension—later evolving to left arm drop while right arm remains extended, with patient adjusting headband using left hand and eventually leaning back with left arm resting on chair; or (2) bilateral tonic stiffening with asynchronous leg movements; or (3) bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, then bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. The patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout the seizure.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial left arm flexion (in three episodes) or extension (in one episode), then evolution into bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Tonic phase characterized by generalized stiffening, followed by clonic phase with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, progressing to asynchronous shaking in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension or flexion; right arm remains extended in some instances. Left leg may extend and lift off the chair, while right leg shows rhythmic clonic jerks in certain episodes. Some events evolve into bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, then bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue. The patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout the episode, with legs exhibiting asynchronous shaking.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs; patient remains unresponsive throughout. In one instance, rightward head version with right arm extension occurred while the left arm remained flexed, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving both arms and legs, with the body arching backward during the seizure.
Right arm extension followed by bilateral tonic stiffening; patient then exhibits asynchronous shaking of both arms. Left arm shows more pronounced flexion compared to the right.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm extension or flexion; subsequently, bilateral tonic stiffening or tonic–clonic movements develop. Asynchronous shaking of both arms and legs may occur. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by sudden upward arm movements and leftward head version. Left arm flexes while right arm remains extended, with subsequent chaotic, asynchronous limb flailing. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion, right arm extension, and left leg extension with uncovering; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in the arms and left leg. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks bilaterally. Head turns left as jerking continues. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by right arm extension with left arm flexion and head turning to the left; rapid card throwing and bilateral tonic stiffening ensue. Patient sits up abruptly, then exhibits bilateral thrashing with asynchronous flailing of the arms as cards scatter. Unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by alternating arm posturing—left arm flexion with right arm extension, and vice versa—accompanied by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Head deviation occurs to both left and right sides during the episodes, with increased jerking intensity. Automatisms include repetitive hand movements.

Rightward head version with right arm extension and left arm flexion, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements may ensue. Additional observations include blank stare preceding left arm flexion and right arm extension, or leftward head version with left arm flexion, with subsequent bilateral or left-limited rhythmic clonic jerks. Automatisms including hand movements near the face are noted in some episodes. Attempts to stabilize the patient's movements were made during one event.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing motor signs, including leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension, was observed. In some instances, these features were followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no further evolution noted in certain events.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing features: either leftward head version with left arm flexion or rightward head version with right arm extension. In multiple instances, these features are followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements, during which the patient remains unresponsive and eyes remain open. One instance notes additional subtle lip smacking with left arm remaining flexed and right arm showing slight extension, without further evolution. In another, a man in white approaches as the patient lifts their right hand to their face following leftward head version and left arm flexion.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; later, rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout and subsequently sits up abruptly, displaying bilateral upper limb movements.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and mild left arm flexion, while the right arm remains extended. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. No further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare followed by left arm raising and lowering; returns to initial posture. No other movements observed.

Blank stare followed by subtle rightward head version. Right arm shows mild extension with subtle clonic jerks; left arm remains still. Head remains centered throughout. Patient appears unresponsive with no further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare followed by subtle rightward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout. No other overt motor signs observed.

Rightward head version with right arm extension, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic activity; unresponsive throughout. No observable signs of seizure activity are present during the remaining periods, with the patient remaining motionless throughout the video.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and/or subtle lip smacking. Left arm flexion observed, sometimes accompanied by right arm extension. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in some episodes. No further evolution or additional signs observed in certain instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by head version and unilateral arm movement—either flexion or extension on the left or right side—preceding the onset of bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout the event, with eyes open and intermittent clonic jerks involving both arms as motor activity intensifies.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended in many instances. Some episodes show only subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion and no further evolution. In several cases, the initial focal features progress to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic-clonic movements, which may be asynchronous, particularly involving the left arm initially before spreading. A subset of episodes evolves with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally without a clear tonic phase. In some observations, tonic-clonic movements ensue with ongoing unresponsiveness throughout all phases. One atypical episode involves rightward head version and right arm extension without further evolution. Across all segments, the patient remains unresponsive during each event.
Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; left arm remains flexed while right arm shows slight extension. No further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; left arm remains flexed while right arm shows mild flexion in one instance and extension in another. No further evolution or spread observed, and patient remains unresponsive throughout.

The patient exhibits a brief period of right arm extension followed by a sudden loud vocalization, then returns to baseline posture without further observable movements. Additionally, there is a blank stare accompanied by subtle left leg movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; in some instances, asymmetric limb involvement is noted, including right arm extension or right arm remaining still. Bilateral tonic stiffening, with both arms extending, ensues in certain episodes, and later evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic or rhythmic clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, and the eyes remain open during the seizures.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor features including left arm flexion and right arm extension, with bilateral tonic stiffening in some episodes. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in all episodes, with head remaining midline in one instance. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by head version (leftward in most instances, one rightward) and asymmetric limb involvement, typically manifesting as left arm extension or flexion; in several episodes, this progressed to bilateral tonic–clonic activity with rhythmic clonic jerks. One episode featured isolated left arm extension with asynchronous shaking while the right arm remained relaxed. In another instance, subtle leftward head version was accompanied by left arm extension and slight right arm flexion without further evolution. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all observed events.

Blank stare followed by bilateral arm elevation with hands open; arms then lower and rest on abdomen. Subtle leftward head version noted. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension or maintained extension observed in some instances. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, either synchronously or asynchronously, evolving from initial focal features. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements occur in some episodes, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout all events. In isolated observations, left hand lifts toward the chest or gently touches the chest without further motor evolution. Subtle lip smacking accompanies some episodes, with sustained left arm flexion and mild right arm extension but no progression to bilateral clonic activity. During certain events, when the left hand is externally held, it remains still despite clinical suspicion of ongoing seizure activity. No significant lateralization changes or additional signs are noted in these cases. All episodes are characterized by unresponsiveness without recovery between manifestations.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, observed in multiple episodes, with variable progression: some evolving to rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm, others progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, and several showing no further evolution. - In certain instances, blank stare was followed by isolated left arm flexion or extension, sometimes with right arm extension or mild flexion, without progression. - Subtle motor signs included left hand movements, abdominal movements, head nodding, and subtle head version (leftward or rightward), often without further evolution. - Asynchronous shaking or rhythmic jerking of the left arm occurred in some episodes, with or without preceding head version. - Bilateral rhythmic jerks or tonic-clonic movements developed in several observations following initial unilateral signs. - Rightward head version with right arm extension or mild flexion and contralateral arm involvement (flexed or still) was noted in a minority of episodes. - In some cases, gradual extension of the left arm occurred with the right arm remaining flexed, without further progression. - Patient remained unresponsive throughout all observed events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with or without right arm extension, observed in the majority of episodes. Subtle left arm flexion or mild leftward head version occurred in some instances, occasionally without further progression. In several cases, nurse intervention involved adjusting the patient’s arm or nearby equipment, with no effect on seizure evolution. Most events progressed to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic–clonic movements, which were sometimes asynchronous. Rightward head version with right arm extension and persistent left arm flexion was noted in a minority of episodes, also progressing to bilateral clonic or tonic–clonic activity. One episode began with right arm extension followed by left arm flexion before generalized seizure activity. Throughout all observations, the patient remained unresponsive, with no additional signs or post-ictal behaviors reported.

Patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with left arm flexion or maintained flexion; right arm may also demonstrate initial flexion prior to extension. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks that progress asynchronously. Head turning to the left is observed during continued jerking in some episodes. Throughout all events, bilateral tonic–clonic movements are present and the patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing motor signs including head version and asymmetric arm posturing (left or right arm flexion or extension), often evolving into bilateral tonic stiffening with subsequent clonic jerks. Clonic activity may begin unilaterally (typically left arm) and spread asymmetrically. In some episodes, the patient sits up abruptly before becoming unresponsive and collapsing with flaccid extremities. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements occur in multiple events, with asynchronous clonic jerks frequently emerging from the initially involved side. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no preservation of awareness or responsiveness during or immediately after the motor phenomena.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; right arm may extend or show intermittent flexion. Bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue, with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. Patient becomes unresponsive, eyes remain open, and may have arms raised above head during the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity characterized by left arm flexion or extension with contralateral right arm extension; progression to bilateral tonic stiffening or bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks, often asynchronous and more prominent on the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, with head turning rightward during the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, then right arm extension with concurrent head turning to the right; alternating arm posturing includes right arm flexion with left arm extension. Bilateral tonic stiffening occurs with asynchronous shaking, followed by intermittent bilateral leg movements. Rhythmic clonic jerks ensue. Patient transitions into a fetal position and remains unresponsive throughout, initially lying supine with arms extended.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity including leftward head version, left arm flexion, and right arm extension; subsequently, bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks progress to tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes open during the episode.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity: initial presentation includes leftward head version with left arm extension or flexion and contralateral arm extension (right arm extension with left arm flexion in one instance). Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop, persisting during the event, with head turning either left or right. The left arm remains flexed throughout in at least one episode. Patient becomes unresponsive with eyes open wide during the events.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity, including left arm flexion and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout the event.

Right arm extension with hand opening; left arm remains still. Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Right arm extension followed by rhythmic clonic jerks; left arm remains flexed. Patient's head turns slightly left as jerks continue.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric limb movements, including unilateral arm extension with contralateral arm flexion and head version to one side, progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive early in the event, with continued tonic–clonic activity involving both arms and evolving head deviation. Seizure manifestations include initial focal features that rapidly generalize, maintaining unresponsiveness throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient becomes unresponsive as movements intensify and remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Right arm flexion followed by left arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Head turns left as jerks continue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue. Patient exhibits rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks occur following asymmetric arm movements, with either left arm flexion and right arm extension or right arm flexion and left arm extension. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode, and the head remains midline.

Right arm extension with left arm flexion, followed by a blank stare and left arm flexion, progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, becoming asynchronous as head turns to the left; patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Head turns left as jerks continue.
Right arm flexion followed by left arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Head turns left as jerks continue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent bilateral clonic jerks ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Rightward head version with right arm extension; left arm remains flexed. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by either rightward head version with right arm extension or left arm extension with right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Right arm raises and extends with left arm flexion, accompanied by rhythmic jerking bilaterally; patient sits up abruptly, then lies back down with arms crossed over chest. Right arm flexion followed by rhythmic hand movements also observed, with continued unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by head version (leftward or rightward) and left arm flexion in some instances; bilateral tonic-clonic movements develop in certain episodes, while others show no further evolution. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion or extension; right arm extension with subtle clonic jerks. Subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm movement, either flexion or extension. Right arm remains extended initially, with subsequent bilateral tonic stiffening in some episodes. Patient may roll to the left side with continued tonic posturing. In one instance, following right arm extension and leftward head version, the patient sat up abruptly and reached for a remote control without further seizure activity. No further evolution observed in other instances.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; right arm raises and extends above the head, then returns to rest, accompanied by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right hand; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains extended; this progresses to right arm extension and left arm flexion, with subsequent development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient then leans forward abruptly. Left arm remains flexed as head returns to center. Patient appears unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed.
Rightward head version with right arm extension; patient then exhibits rhythmic jerks of the right arm. Left arm remains flexed throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient then exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks in both arms.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version. Left arm flexes while right arm remains extended. No further evolution observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version. Left arm flexes while right arm extends. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; nurse approaches as patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks in the left hand.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; left arm shows mild flexion or extension. Right arm extension with left arm flexion may occur, sometimes accompanied by head turning to the right. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, with asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally, often spreading from the left side to both arms. At times, the patient raises both arms above the head with hands open. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric arm posturing (left arm flexion or extension with right arm extension), then evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic or rhythmic clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no observable signs in one segment and consistent seizure activity across others without changes in posture or movement in that instance.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with subsequent development of rhythmic clonic jerks that may remain focal in the left arm or progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm movements, including rightward head version with right arm extension or leftward head version with left arm flexion or extension. Bilateral tonic-clonic movements develop in most episodes, characterized by tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks. Shaking is often asynchronous, with one side more prominently involved initially, sometimes spreading contralaterally. Automatisms are observed during some events. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with loss of responsiveness coinciding with seizure progression.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension; subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic or rhythmic clonic movements develop. No other signs of seizure activity are observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare accompanied by lateralizing signs including leftward or rightward head version and corresponding arm involvement. Leftward head version associated with left arm flexion or extension, subtle clonic jerks of the left arm, and leaning forward and to the left; during these episodes, the left arm remains either flexed or extended while the right arm is not visibly active. Rightward head version observed with right arm extension. Additional features include subtle lip smacking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with no further evolution or additional signs observed in any episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with persistence of an open-eyed state throughout; patient becomes unresponsive as seizures continue. Initial episode noted with only left arm flexion and subsequent sitting upright with clothing adjustment, without clonic jerks or tonic stiffening.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with concurrent right arm flexion and left arm extension; patient then exhibits bilateral tonic stiffening with subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements that are more pronounced on the left side. Asynchronous shaking occurs during the clonic phase. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the entire episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and simultaneous left arm flexion or extension, with right arm extension noted in one instance; evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements in all cases. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes remaining open during the episode.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and mild left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended. Additional features include subtle lip smacking in some instances. The episodes progress in some cases to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. No further evolution or additional signs were observed in all instances.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with some segments noting left arm flexion. Right arm extension is followed by the patient reaching out with the right hand toward nearby objects or a device, without further evolution of movements in some instances. Subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic movements occur, along with bilateral arm movements and the patient leaning forward to hold the head with both hands. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode in certain observations, while in others, no clear early signs of seizure activity are noted, including absence of tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms, and the patient remains stationary with head down and hands covering the face.

- Blank stare observed in multiple instances, occasionally followed by subtle lip smacking or brief eye opening; patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes. - Leftward head version noted in association with blank stare, with or without concurrent left arm flexion or right arm extension. - No further evolution of seizure activity observed in any episode; no additional automatisms or motor signs present beyond those described. - Patient remains motionless with no observable seizure signs during several segments of the observation.

- Blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking, with or without slight left arm flexion and right arm extension; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent evolution to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the left arm alone or progressing bilaterally. Some episodes showed no further evolution beyond initial focal motor signs, characterized by brief left arm flexion after the blank stare and head version, but still accompanied by persistent unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; the seizure evolves with either focal motor activity (rhythmic clonic jerks, predominantly in the left arm) or progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases of the seizure.

- Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; patient remains motionless and unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; head remains centered, with no significant limb movements or tonic stiffening observed. In others, no further evolution is observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

- Blank stare followed by variable initial manifestations including subtle lip smacking, left arm flexion, or leftward head version; patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes. - Initial left arm flexion is commonly observed, sometimes accompanied by right arm extension; in some cases, left arm extension occurs instead. - Leftward head version frequently accompanies left arm involvement (flexion or extension), with occasional subtle or mild head turning without further progression. - In several instances, left arm flexion progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the left arm; these may remain unilateral or evolve into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. - Bilateral motor evolution is common, characterized by tonic stiffening followed by asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks, often emerging after initial unilateral signs. - Bilateral tonic–clonic movements typically follow a focal onset with leftward head version and ipsilateral arm involvement (flexion or extension), indicating secondary generalization. - Automatisms such as repetitive hand gestures and lip smacking occur in some episodes, associated with preserved unresponsiveness. - Mouth opening widely is noted during select episodes, particularly those involving generalized motor activity. - Head turning to the left may persist or emerge during ongoing clonic activity. - Some episodes show no further evolution beyond the initial blank stare with subtle motor signs (e.g., mild limb flexion or head version), remaining non-generalized.

Blank stare is consistently observed, followed by variable motor manifestations. Episodes include left arm flexion or extension, often accompanied by rhythmic clonic jerking that may remain focal or spread to involve the contralateral side bilaterally. Leftward head version frequently accompanies left arm involvement, with patterns of ipsilateral flexion or extension. In some episodes, right arm extension persists without further progression. Bilateral clonic jerking develops in several instances following initial unilateral signs. Subtle manifestations include brief hand movements and mild limb posturing without evolution. One episode features isolated lip smacking without additional motor signs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with eyes closed during motor activity and no verbal responsiveness noted.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—initially affecting the left side, with subsequent evolution to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic–clonic activity in most episodes. Left arm flexion is the most common initial motor manifestation, often accompanied by leftward head version. In several instances, rhythmic clonic jerks begin in the left arm and may remain lateralized or progress to bilateral involvement, including both arms and legs. Some events feature asymmetric onset with left arm flexion and right arm extension before becoming bilateral. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements develop in multiple observations, typically following a focal onset. One episode began with rhythmic jaw movements and asymmetric limb posturing before evolving into generalized convulsive activity. Right leg clonic movements are intermittently observed in one instance, and in another, clonic jerking involves the left leg after initial left arm and head version. Throughout all episodes, the patient remains unresponsive, with eyes open during at least one event. No progression beyond bilateral convulsive activity is noted in the majority, though a few events show no further evolution beyond initial unilateral clonic jerks.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, which in most cases progressed to bilateral tonic stiffening with subsequent asynchronous clonic jerks that spread from the left side to involve both arms or all limbs. Some episodes featured left arm extension instead of flexion. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements were commonly observed, with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and intermittent eye closure during the seizure. The patient became unresponsive in all instances and remained so throughout the events.
