One episode involved only left arm flexion without further evolution, after which the patient reached for a water bottle, drank, and returned it to the table, with no additional seizure activity noted.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity including unilateral arm flexion and contralateral arm extension, often accompanied by head version to the side of the arm flexion. Episodes may begin with leftward or rightward head version and corresponding arm movements: left arm flexion with right arm extension or vice versa. This initial phase is frequently followed by bilateral tonic stiffening, evolving into rhythmic clonic jerks that may be asynchronous and predominantly affect one side, particularly the left. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements also occur, with subsequent unresponsiveness throughout the event. The patient remains unresponsive during all phases, with no purposeful activity. Following the motor manifestations, the patient returns to a supine position, sometimes with arms crossed over the chest. In one instance, the patient sits up abruptly, leans forward, and presses a button after the initial motor signs, but ultimately loses responsiveness as the episode progresses.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or left arm flexion with right arm extension; initial focal motor signs are consistent across multiple episodes. - Following the initial manifestation, variable secondary generalization is observed: in some episodes, bilateral tonic–clonic movements develop with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying supine, occasionally with arms crossed over chest; rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally in multiple instances, with sustained unresponsiveness throughout. - Postural and behavioral evolution varies: patient may sit upright with hands clasped, lie back with arms extended, turn to prone position with arms crossed over chest, or sit up abruptly with rightward head tilt and right arm extension; in several cases, no further tonic or clonic activity follows. - Asynchronous shaking of limbs occurs in one episode after assuming a supine position. - One atypical episode features isolated right leg lifting and extension with rhythmic movement of the left hand, without other clear seizure signs.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension or bilateral arm involvement, with leftward head version frequently observed. Tonic stiffening and bilateral tonic–clonic movements develop, often preceded by asymmetric limb posturing such as right arm extension with clenched fist and left arm flexion. Rhythmic clonic jerks emerge bilaterally, sometimes asynchronous and more pronounced on the left side, progressing from arms to legs. Patient becomes unresponsive during seizure activity, which may include abrupt sitting up or sustained unresponsiveness throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, or right arm extension with left arm flexion, sometimes accompanied by leftward head version; bilateral tonic–clonic or rhythmic clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with legs occasionally exhibiting asynchronous shaking. In one instance, patient sits up abruptly during the event but continues to display unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally; patient remains unresponsive as movements intensify. In one instance, right arm extension with left arm flexion preceded the action, during which the patient grabbed a can and drank; later, turned head left and held a blue object near mouth. Another episode featured right arm extension with clenched fist, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg, with the left arm remaining flexed and the head turning left. Additionally, an event began with blank stare, leftward head version, and left arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with persistent unresponsiveness.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. In another episode, blank stare is followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with bilateral tonic stiffening and asynchronous clonic jerks spreading from arms to legs. Clonic jerks also occur initially in both arms and legs, becoming more pronounced and widespread, with asynchronous shaking persisting throughout. Additionally, right arm extension is observed, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks isolated to the right arm, without head version or automatisms. The patient remains unresponsive during all events.

Blank stare followed by subtle head version, either leftward or rightward. During leftward version, left arm shows mild flexion while the right remains still. During rightward version, right arm exhibits mild extension or brief extension; patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, evolving into bilateral tonic stiffening and subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements with asynchronous shaking predominantly affecting the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; patient then covers face with both hands. Asynchronous shaking ensues, predominantly affecting the upper limbs. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric focal onset with either left arm flexion and right arm extension or left arm extension and right arm flexion; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks involving all extremities. Arms flail asynchronously during the tonic-clonic phase. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric arm posturing, characterized by left arm extension with right arm flexion or bilateral extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. Rhythmic clonic jerks intensify bilaterally, during which the patient becomes unresponsive. Eyes remain open throughout, and the mouth opens intermittently. Oxygen mask applied as seizure continues.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, with subsequent development of bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Rhythmic jerks emerge bilaterally, and the patient becomes unresponsive as the seizure progresses, remaining so throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by left arm extension and leftward head version; right arm extension with left arm flexion observed in multiple episodes, often accompanied by head turning to the right. Bilateral tonic stiffening or flexion–extension posturing ensues, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks that may begin asymmetrically and spread. Tonic–clonic movements progress with asynchronous or synchronous jerking involving both arms. Patient becomes unresponsive during all episodes and remains so throughout the event; one episode concludes with abrupt sitting up and arms raised.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; right arm may extend or remain flexed. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, evolving into asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks that may spread from the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with eyes closed during events.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion, followed by blank stare and leftward head version; bilateral tonic stiffening occurs, and rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; left arm shows mild flexion initially in some episodes, with one instance of rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the left arm assisted by a woman. Subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic movements emerge, accompanied by right arm extension and left arm flexion, evolving into rhythmic clonic jerks bilaterally. Head turns rightward during later phases, with automatisms including hand movements and lip smacking. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with eyes remaining open during bilateral tonic-clonic activity. No further evolution observed in certain phases.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and variable involvement of the left arm, including flexion or extension. In some instances, this progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks isolated to the left arm, while in others, bilateral clonic or bilateral tonic-clonic activity ensues. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes. No further evolution observed in certain events.
Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm remains flexed while right arm stays extended. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and asymmetric motor activity with right arm extension and left arm flexion initially; patient briefly reaches out with left hand but no further evolution of movements noted. Later, bilateral tonic-clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; right arm remains extended. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity, including left arm flexion with right arm extension. Bilateral tonic stiffening occurs with arms extended and hands clenched, progressing to asynchronous clonic jerks that spread from upper to lower limbs. Rhythmic clonic jerks evolve into generalized bilateral tonic-clonic movements, preceded by initial flailing of both arms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion. Right arm remains extended initially. Asynchronous shaking develops bilaterally.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension. Patient then transitions to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
Blank stare, followed by subtle leftward head version. No further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking. No further evolution or laterality observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare, followed by subtle leftward head version. No further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking. No further evolution or laterality observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. The patient remains unresponsive throughout. A nurse enters and exits the frame.
Blank stare, followed by left arm flexion. No further evolution observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension. Rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm develop.
Blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version. No further evolution or additional signs observed.
Blank stare, followed by subtle leftward head version. No other movements or automatisms observed. Patient remains still throughout.
Blank stare, followed by subtle leftward head version. No further evolution or additional signs observed.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; right arm remains extended. Rhythmic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient unresponsive throughout. - Right arm flexion with left arm extension observed initially; patient points to right arm with left hand. No further evolution or additional signs noted.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; then bilateral tonic stiffening ensues. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing signs including head version and arm posturing: rightward head version with right arm extension (or sustained extension) observed in multiple episodes, sometimes accompanied by left arm flexion or maintained flexion; leftward head version with left arm flexion or extension noted in other episodes. Some events evolved to bilateral tonic–clonic activity with asynchronous shaking, while others featured rhythmic clonic jerks limited to the left arm or no further evolution beyond the initial focal features. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes.

- Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version observed in multiple episodes, with variable accompanying motor signs. - In some instances, leftward head version was accompanied by left arm flexion and/or right arm extension; in others, left arm gradual extension occurred with right arm remaining flexed. - Left arm flexion was frequently observed, sometimes in isolation, and occasionally with right arm extension or bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks developing later. - Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks occurred in several episodes following initial blank stare and asymmetric motor onset (e.g., left arm flexion ± head version), with asynchronous shaking beginning in the left arm and spreading bilaterally in one instance. - Patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes in which responsiveness was specified. - One episode featured patient sitting up abruptly after blank stare and leftward head version with left arm flexion, during which the patient received and examined a pink object while seated. - Subtle lip smacking occurred in one episode, followed by patient adjusting a book and briefly touching mouth, with no further motor activity. - No further evolution or additional signs were noted in several episodes, particularly those involving only subtle head version without limb involvement.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing features including leftward or rightward head version and corresponding arm movements—such as left arm extension or flexion, right arm extension, or asymmetric posturing (e.g., left arm flexion with right arm extension)—preceding bilateral motor manifestations. These evolving signs are consistently followed by either bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks. In some instances, right arm remains extended during the event. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. In one instance, the right arm remained extended with asynchronous shaking that evolved into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and/or asymmetric arm posturing (left arm flexion with or without right arm extension), progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. In some episodes, the right arm remains extended. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, then right arm flexion with progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Right arm extension with left arm flexion, accompanied by a blank stare; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient's eyes remain closed throughout, and the head turns to the left as the jerking continues.
Blank stare followed by left arm extension; patient reaches out with left hand. A nurse approaches as the patient maintains extended posture.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; later, bilateral tonic–clonic activity or rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. Right arm extension with left arm flexion; patient reaches out with right hand. Subsequently, bilateral hand movements and head turning occur, with adjustment of head covering.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, then progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. In some instances, bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue with asynchronous shaking of both arms. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. Occasionally, blank stare is followed by isolated left arm flexion or subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion while the right arm stays extended; rhythmic clonic jerking may be localized to the left arm or become bilateral. In a few observations, right arm extension precedes left arm flexion, sometimes accompanied by rhythmic hand movements or upward pointing with no further evolution. Bilateral rhythmic jerking may still develop. One episode features leftward head version and left arm extension without further evolution; in others, this combination progresses to bilateral rhythmic jerks or clonic activity, with the right arm remaining extended or still. Patient consistently remains unresponsive during all observed events. Facial covering with both hands occurs during ongoing bilateral jerking in one instance. No additional motor evolution is noted in several episodes.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and either right arm extension or bilateral tonic–clonic movements; in some instances, lip smacking occurs. The left arm may remain still or be assisted by a nurse holding it steady, while the right arm extends in certain episodes. During some events, the patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout, with progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. In one instance, the patient began drinking from a cup after left arm flexion, with no further evolution of movements and preserved responsiveness.

Blank stare followed by lateralized motor signs including leftward or rightward head version, and focal onset in the upper extremities—manifesting as arm extension, flexion, or subtle rhythmic jerking. These initial focal features may involve the left or right arm alone or asymmetrically, with early contralateral involvement in some cases. Focal onset is consistently followed by evolution to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic-clonic movements. Lip smacking is observed in one instance as an initial subtle sign, accompanied by brief left arm flexion. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no variation in level of consciousness reported across observations.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm extension or flexion, with subsequent development of bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity, including arm extension and/or flexion with or without head version to the left; rhythmic clonic jerks involve the left arm or generalize bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout each episode and holds a plush toy during seizures.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally, with initial extension of the right arm and persistent flexion of the left. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later bilateral tonic–clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and simultaneous involvement of the left arm, manifesting as flexion or extension with occasional brief rhythmic jerks; later progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements is observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the entire episode.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; later, bilateral tonic–clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare observed in all episodes, consistently followed by leftward head version or subtle head movement, with variable motor manifestations. Left arm involvement included extension, flexion, or sustained tonic posturing, sometimes accompanied by rhythmic clonic jerks isolated to the left arm. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks developed in multiple episodes following initial focal signs. In one episode, sudden eye opening with upward gaze and brief leftward head turn occurred without further motor activity. Another episode featured subtle eye fluttering with left arm extension and slight twitching of the right arm. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

- Blank stare, followed by subtle leftward head version. No further signs of seizure activity observed in several episodes. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; no other movements observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout multiple occurrences. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm remaining extended in some instances. Evolution varies: in some cases, no further progression is noted; in others, bilateral tonic–clonic or rhythmic clonic movements develop, including bilateral tonic stiffening. - Episodes consistently characterized by unresponsiveness throughout.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, evolving into asynchronous shaking or rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm or hand; progression to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic-clonic movements observed in multiple episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with or without subsequent bilateral tonic-clonic movements; left arm remains flexed in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion alone, with variable evolution: rhythmic clonic jerks of the left hand, rhythmic jaw movements, intermittent raising of the right arm, or no further progression. In one instance, patient reaches out with left hand and drinks from a cup while maintaining left arm flexion. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and mild left arm flexion transitioning to rhythmic clonic jerks, without further spread. - Right arm extension followed by bilateral tonic stiffening without further evolution; patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended. Subtle lip smacking may be present initially. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, progressing to asynchronous shaking or generalized tonic–clonic movements. The patient becomes unresponsive and remains so throughout the episode. No further evolution or additional signs are observed in some instances.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains extended. Subsequently, right arm extension occurs with persistent left arm flexion, and asynchronous shaking begins in the right arm. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare accompanied by subtle lip smacking or subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild to maintained flexion, while the right arm exhibits slight extension or remains extended; in one instance, no significant head version or limb movements were observed. No further evolution or additional signs noted across all episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

- Blank stare, followed by subtle lip smacking. No significant head version or limb movements observed in some episodes; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally in one episode. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Subtle leftward head version with left arm mild flexion and right arm extended or showing slight extension observed across multiple episodes; no further evolution or additional signs in most cases. - One episode of blank stare followed by leftward head version with left arm flexed and right arm extended, without progression. - Patient drinks from cup and places it down in one interval; no seizure activity observed, remains still and unresponsive.

- Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; left arm remains flexed while right arm extends or shows slight extension. No further evolution or additional signs observed in multiple instances. - Blank stare followed by right arm extension; patient reaches for a remote control. No other movements observed. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm gradually extends or shows mild flexion while the right arm remains flexed or extended. No further evolution or additional signs observed across several episodes. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking, without significant head version or limb movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended or showing slight extension in some instances. No further evolution or additional signs observed throughout; patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare accompanied by subtle leftward head version and mild left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended; in some instances, the left arm gradually extends while the right arm remains flexed. Lip smacking is noted in one episode. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in one observation, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. No further evolution or additional signs are observed in the remaining episodes.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, initially brief and later accompanied by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

- Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with or without subsequent evolution into bilateral tonic-clonic activity or bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; later evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic activity; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; later evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic activity; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version; left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains extended; no further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity, including unilateral arm flexion and contralateral arm extension, with progression to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking. Head version may occur toward the side of initial involvement. Seizure evolution includes eye widening, mouth opening, and spread of motor activity from one hemisphere to both sides, often starting unilaterally and becoming generalized. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient then adjusts posture with both arms.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right remains still. No further evolution, automatisms, or additional signs observed; patient remains still throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head tilt and left arm flexion; patient writes intermittently.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; a person approaches and interacts with the patient's arm.
Right arm extension with upward gaze; patient adjusts glasses with right hand. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by sudden awakening with the patient sitting up abruptly and holding a remote control; no clonic jerks or tonic stiffening observed. Recurrent episodes characterized by blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with variable involvement of the right arm: either extended, flexed, or remaining in neutral. Some events begin with left arm flexion and right arm extension. Following the initial focal features, several episodes progress to bilateral motor involvement: tonic stiffening with asynchronous clonic jerks, or evolution into bilateral rhythmic tonic–clonic activity involving both arms and legs. During these generalized phases, the patient is unresponsive. A woman approaches and interacts with the patient during one of the episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient then sits up abruptly with continued left arm flexion while picking up a remote control, after which no further movements occur. In another instance, the same initial features are followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks spreading from arms to legs, with extension of the left arm observed in one episode. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements also ensue in separate episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and/or leftward head version with left arm flexion; patient holds a plush toy tightly in some instances. Rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally in one episode. A nurse assists in uncovering the patient's legs, revealing no further movements. No clear evolution of motor activity in all episodes, and patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial involvement of the left arm, manifesting as either flexion or extension, then progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm variably flexed or extended. Left arm extension may occur initially in some instances. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop either locally in the left arm or generalize bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and/or subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains still; rightward head version occurs briefly in some episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement, manifesting as either flexion or extension. In episodes with left arm flexion, bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks subsequently develop; the right arm may remain extended during these events. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking. Left arm gradually flexes while the right arm remains extended. No significant evolution or further laterality observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
The patient initially displays a blank stare with hands covering the face. Hands are then lowered, revealing a neutral expression. The patient remains still and unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; in some episodes, this progressed to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In others, no further evolution or additional signs were observed. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events. One episode involved only a subtle leftward head version with brief left arm flexion before returning to rest.
Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion. No further evolution observed.
Blank stare, followed by left arm flexion. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm involvement—either leftward with left arm flexion or extension, or rightward with right arm extension and left arm flexion—progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The tonic phase includes bilateral stiffening, followed by clonic jerks that are often asynchronous and may predominate on one side, particularly the left. Rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs is observed during the clonic phase. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion or extension, with concurrent right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop in the left arm, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes remaining open during the episodes.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks bilaterally. Head turns rightward during the episode.

Blank stare followed by sudden vocalization and bilateral arm flailing, with leftward head version and left arm flexion progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. A woman approaches briefly. Continuous vocalizations and motor activity persist throughout, and the patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor features including left arm flexion with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by sudden bilateral arm elevation with clenched fists; a person approaches and interacts, but the patient remains unresponsive. Right arm extension with left arm flexion ensues, and rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, with arms remaining elevated and exhibiting intermittent movements throughout.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Head turns right as jerks continue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by bilateral arm extension and upward gaze, with tonic stiffening progressing to rhythmic clonic jerks in both arms; in one instance, the left arm showed rhythmic clonic jerks while the right remained extended. One episode featured isolated right arm extension with left hand grasping and no other movements. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by right arm extension with left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral arm extension with fingers splayed; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, and a woman approaches as the jerking continues.
Right arm extension with a blank stare; leftward head version follows. No further evolution observed.

Right arm flexion with left arm extended; patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms, intermittently involving the right arm alone. Head remains midline throughout, with no other limb involvement or automatisms observed. Patient appears unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with either right arm extension or no specified right arm movement; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension in most instances; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Subtle leftward head version frequently observed, often accompanying mild left arm flexion and persistent right arm extension; no further evolution or additional signs in several episodes. - Blank stare with subtle lip smacking and persistent left arm flexion with slight right arm extension noted across multiple events; no progression or additional motor activity observed. - Some episodes begin with blank stare and isolated left arm flexion, with or without head version; patient remains unresponsive. - Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop in a subset of episodes following initial asymmetric features (left arm flexion, right arm extension, leftward head version); these evolve into bilateral tonic-clonic movements in some cases. - Right leg extension with rhythmic clonic jerking occurs in isolation during some episodes, occasionally with intermittent right arm flexion; left leg remains relatively still and no head version or automatisms are observed. - Head remains midline in select episodes with bilateral clonic jerking and asymmetric arm posturing (left flexion, right extension). - Automatisms such as repetitive lip smacking, left hand covering the mouth, or wiping the mouth with a tissue occur during some episodes; patient remains unresponsive. - One episode includes external intervention (man adjusts patient’s head, another holds left hand), but no change in core seizure semiology is observed. - Brief pauses between jerks noted in right leg during one episode; patient unresponsive. - A few episodes show only subtle leftward head version or minimal motor signs after blank stare, with no further evolution. - No seizure progression observed in multiple segments, maintaining focal onset characteristics without secondary generalization.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with subtle flexion noted; patient then exhibits bilateral tonic-clonic movements and is unresponsive throughout the event. Doctor's hand briefly touches patient's chest.

Blank stare is consistently observed, often followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, which frequently progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm or bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. In multiple instances, these motor manifestations evolve into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Some segments show initial rightward head version with mild right arm extension or isolated right leg clonic jerking while the left leg remains still; in one case, left leg extension and right arm movement are noted, followed by purposeful actions such as sitting upright, removing socks, kneeling, and reaching for items. Subtle lip smacking occurs in association with blank stare in some episodes, and a few events include the patient touching the face with the left hand or adjusting a headband with the right hand. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events, with hands sometimes clasped, holding a device, bottle, or stuffed toy during the episodes. A nurse applies a wristband during one episode in which the left arm remains flexed. No further evolution of movements is reported in several instances, and postural changes are generally absent except where noted.

Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking and brief rightward or leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion and patient briefly touches face with left hand; right arm remains still. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, then progression to rightward head version with right arm extension while the left arm remains flexed; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm evolve into bilateral clonic jerking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Right arm extension with subtle clonic jerks develops, while the left arm remains flexed; no lip smacking or further head movement initially observed. The activity evolves to rightward head version with bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Movement remains localized to the arms without spreading, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Right arm extension with rhythmic clonic jerks occurs as jerks continue asynchronously, while the left arm remains flexed and the head turns slightly left. Bilateral tonic-clonic movements may ensue. In some instances, only subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion with maintained right arm extension are observed, without further evolution. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with subsequent development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout the episodes.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; woman leans over and adjusts patient's head. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; man approaches and interacts. Patient exhibits sudden rightward head turn and right arm extension.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm ensue. The patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare is consistently observed as the initial manifestation, followed in most instances by left arm flexion. In several episodes, this progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm or hand, with subsequent evolution to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Leftward head version accompanies some events featuring left arm involvement. Subtle lip smacking and right arm flexion are noted in isolated observations, with one episode showing bilateral hand movements progressing to synchronous clonic jerking of both arms. Throughout all events, the patient remains unresponsive. Not all episodes show progression beyond initial focal features; in one case, only slight left arm flexion occurs without further spread.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity characterized by initial right arm extension with left arm flexion, evolving into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks that continue asynchronously. Tonic-clonic movements progress with ongoing unresponsiveness throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor signs including left arm flexion or extension with contralateral (right) arm extension; this progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic or rhythmic clonic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor activity, including left arm flexion and right arm extension, or rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing in some instances to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Rhythmic clonic jerks and asynchronous shaking may occur, predominantly affecting the left arm. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended; bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue, including rhythmic clonic jerks of both arms and legs. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial involvement of the left arm, manifesting as either extension or flexion; subsequently, bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or tonic–clonic movements develop. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by head version and asymmetric arm involvement—either leftward head version with left arm extension or flexion, or rightward head version with right arm extension while the left arm remains flexed—preceding bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Patient exhibits episodes characterized by a blank stare or focal motor onset (right arm flexion or left arm extension) followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks. In several instances, leftward head version accompanies left arm extension prior to bilateral involvement. Head remains midline in some episodes, while slight leftward turning is noted in others. Unresponsiveness is consistently observed throughout all events. Bilateral clonic jerking occurs following asymmetric limb posturing, including combinations of left arm flexion with right arm extension and vice versa. Focal motor onset involving the right leg manifests as isolated rhythmic clonic jerks without involvement of the left leg or other body regions; during these episodes, the patient remains unresponsive with a blank stare and no associated automatisms. No additional movements or signs are reported in the isolated lower limb events.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and variable head version (leftward or rightward); patient adjusts mask with left hand. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing in some instances to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic–clonic movements; patient remains unresponsive throughout these events. - Blank stare followed by left arm extension or isolated left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive. In some cases, left arm flexion is sustained or evolves into rhythmic clonic jerking of the left arm. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking without other motor signs; patient remains unresponsive. - Blank stare followed by right arm flexion or extension, with or without subsequent bilateral clonic jerking; in one instance, right arm extension occurs with subtle clonic jerks while the left arm remains still, eyes closed, without head movement or automatisms. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward or rightward head version; in some cases, associated with mild limb posturing (e.g., left arm flexion or extension, right arm flexion), but without further evolution or automatisms. - Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking develops following various initial signs, including left arm flexion or right arm extension, with patient remaining unresponsive. - One event features a blank stare and left arm flexion during which the patient gives a thumbs-up gesture, with no further progression. - Nursing intervention observed in one instance: patient’s arm adjusted during an event characterized by blank stare and left arm flexion, with brief leftward head version but no clonic jerks or automatisms.

Blank stare followed by head version and arm involvement, either to the left (with left arm extension or flexion) or to the right (with right arm extension). Unilateral rhythmic clonic jerks may involve the left arm, progressing in some instances to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Automatisms include lip smacking and intermittent hand movements. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version, left arm flexion (with or without elevation and brief clenching of the left hand), and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended in some instances. Left arm flexion may occur without head version. In several episodes, rhythmic clonic or bilateral tonic-clonic movements develop after the initial motor signs. Bilateral rhythmic jerks or tonic-clonic activity follows the onset in most cases. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with eyes closed and hands near face observed during one episode. One isolated event consists of blank stare followed only by left hand touching the face, without further motor activity or loss of responsiveness.

- Blank stare followed by variable lateralizing signs including head version (rightward or leftward), asymmetric arm posturing (flexion, extension, or mixed), and unilateral limb movements. - Subtle lip smacking occurs in some episodes without significant motor evolution or change in responsiveness. - Episodes may progress to bilateral tonic–clonic activity, bilateral tonic stiffening, or asynchronous rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. - In some instances, coordinated motor behaviors such as throwing a pillow upward with both arms occur following initial automatisms or focal motor signs. - Head remains centered or midline during some events, particularly those beginning with asymmetric arm movements and evolving to bilateral clonic jerking. - Patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed episodes; loss of awareness is consistent across event types.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, with subsequent bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks developing in most cases. Tonic-clonic movements ensue in several instances, including both arms and legs exhibiting rhythmic jerking. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. In some segments, left arm flexion occurs without progression to bilateral clonic activity, and right arm extension is occasionally noted alongside left arm involvement. Bilateral clonic activity may spread symmetrically when starting asymmetrically. One atypical segment shows the patient raising the left hand to the head with fingers splayed after left arm flexion, breaking from the typical pattern of unresponsiveness. Brief leftward head version may occur without further evolution. No automatisms are observed in any episode.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing motor features, including head version and arm involvement (either flexion or extension) contralateral to the side of head turning. This initial focal onset is consistently followed by evolution to bilateral motor activity, manifesting as either bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks, which may remain asymmetric with prominent involvement of the arm ipsilateral to the initial head version. Consciousness is impaired throughout all events, with no response during any phase of the seizure.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion, often accompanied by leftward head version and right arm extension; bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop symmetrically, progressing to tonic-clonic movements in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the events, except in one case where the patient adjusted the headband with the right hand after left arm flexion, with no further progression.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, progressing to generalized tonic–clonic activity involving both arms and legs. Asynchronous shaking of the arms may occur initially. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; a nurse assists as rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, then sits up abruptly.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity involving the left arm, which may manifest as extension or flexion while the right arm remains extended; this is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Automatisms include repetitive hand movements. Patient adjusts headband with both hands at one point but remains unresponsive throughout the episodes.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; left arm remains flexed. Patient then lifts left arm briefly. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient then lifts left arm and touches chest. Abrupt rightward head version occurs, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements. No further movements observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with asynchronous shaking in some instances; rhythmic clonic jerks may develop bilaterally following initial focal motor signs. Episodes may begin with isolated left arm flexion or with simultaneous left arm flexion and right arm extension. Right arm repetitive flexion and extension movements also occur as an independent pattern, sometimes with subtle clonic jerks, while the left arm remains still. During some events, a dog interacts with the patient’s hand or approaches during the episode. No head version, lip smacking, or other automatisms are observed during right arm-predominant episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with no further evolution noted in certain cases.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion or extension, with subsequent development of rhythmic clonic jerks that may remain unilateral (left arm) or progress to bilateral involvement. In some episodes, right arm extension accompanies left arm flexion prior to bilateral clonic activity. Automatisms in the form of rhythmic hand gestures are observed during certain events. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes. One atypical event features a blank stare with brief leftward head version and left arm flexion without clonic jerks or automatisms.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout. In some episodes, left arm flexion is accompanied by right arm extension, with bilateral clonic movements developing thereafter. Isolated right arm flexion with contralateral extension is also observed, sometimes without head version or automatisms such as lip smacking. Right arm repetitive flexion and extension movements occur independently, with persistent rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the right arm in certain instances. Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks are a recurring feature across events, though in some cases jerking is more prominent on one side. Head remains centered during some sequences, and no automatisms are noted. Patient is consistently unresponsive during all motor activity. Camera angle shifts reveal a tablet and earphones on the table.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with subsequent development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks in multiple episodes; patient remains unresponsive throughout. In some instances, left arm flexion occurs without progression to bilateral movements, and right arm remains extended without evolution. Asynchronous shaking involves the right leg during certain events. One episode features left leg extension and right leg flexion with concurrent left hand automatisms, while another shows subtle clonic jerks in the right leg. Right arm extension with subtle clonic jerks is observed with the left arm remaining flexed, without head version or other automatisms. Bilateral tonic-clonic movements emerge later in several sequences. No further evolution beyond initial posturing is noted in isolated events.

- Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm shows mild flexion while the right arm remains extended. No further evolution or additional signs observed. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking. No significant head version or limb movements observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and left arm involvement, with variable progression. In several instances, the left arm showed mild flexion while the right arm remained extended, without further evolution or additional signs; patient remained unresponsive throughout. One episode featured left arm flexion transitioning into rhythmic clonic jerks. In another, the left arm gradually extended while the right arm remained flexed. No other motor evolution or clinical signs were observed across episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or, in one instance, left arm extension; lip smacking is noted in a single episode without further evolution. In most cases, the episode evolves to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic–clonic movements. One episode showed no progression beyond the initial motor signs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; then bilateral tonic stiffening ensues. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; then bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by lateralizing features including head version (leftward or rightward) and asymmetric arm involvement (left or right arm flexion or extension). Initial focal motor signs progress to bilateral involvement, with rhythmic clonic jerks or tonic–clonic movements spreading from arms to legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with variable involvement of the right arm—ranging from flexion to stillness or subtle movements. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues in most episodes, often progressing to rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic–clonic movements. In some instances, mouth opening widely is observed without clear progression to bilateral involvement or automatisms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension, with subsequent evolution into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, often beginning asymmetrically and spreading from the left side. In some episodes, initial rightward head version and right arm extension are observed before progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Bilateral tonic stiffening is followed by asynchronous clonic jerks, particularly involving the legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with eyes remaining open during at least one episode.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining either flexed or extended. Subtle clonic jerks of the left hand develop in some episodes, occasionally progressing to brief rhythmic jerks of the left arm. In several instances, rhythmic clonic jerks evolve bilaterally. Some episodes show gradual extension of the left arm with the right arm remaining flexed, accompanied by only subtle head version and no further evolution. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events. No further evolution is noted in multiple episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, often with brief or subtle movements initially; in most cases, this evolves into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Left arm flexion may be accompanied by rhythmic clonic jerks localized to the left hand or arm, while the right arm typically remains extended or still. In some instances, only mild or subtle left arm flexion occurs without further evolution. Head version is predominantly leftward, occasionally subtle, and may be associated with slight leftward head turn. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes. Separate events involve the right arm, characterized by flexion, extension, or rhythmic clonic jerks, sometimes with subtle shaking; these are consistently unilateral with no involvement of the left arm and without head version or oral automatisms in several cases. In a subset of right-sided events, head turns slightly left with left arm remaining flexed, and automatisms such as lip smacking, swallowing, or repetitive hand movements are observed. Right arm activity includes intermittent flexion and extension or sustained extension followed by clonic jerking. Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking commonly follows initial focal features, particularly after left-sided onset. Some seizures remain focal with no secondary generalization, showing only regional motor signs without progression.
Blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version. No further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive. Subtle leftward head version observed, with left arm gradually extending while the right arm remains flexed. Patient subsequently covers face with left hand. No further evolution or additional movements observed.

- Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm gradually extends while the right arm remains flexed; no further evolution observed. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version. Left arm flexes briefly; no further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by sudden upward gaze; patient sits up abruptly with arms extended, then leans forward. Returns to lying down with leftward head turn and left arm flexion. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; then bilateral tonic stiffening ensues. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Right arm flexion followed by left arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and leftward head version, progressing in some instances to bilateral tonic–clonic activity; subtle left arm flexion may also occur without progression. Rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm are observed in some episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent evolution in some instances to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In several cases, the seizure began with left arm flexion alone or in combination with right arm extension, sometimes without head version. One episode featured only subtle lip smacking without significant motor signs. Asymmetric limb involvement (left arm flexion with right arm extension) was noted in multiple observations. Bilateral clonic or tonic-clonic activity developed progressively in many episodes, with asynchronous shaking evolving into generalized motor convulsions in at least one case. One atypical presentation included left arm extension instead of flexion following head version, with clonic jerking limited to the left arm. The patient remained unresponsive during the majority of events; in one instance, responsiveness was lost as the seizure progressed. Brief self-protective behavior (covering face with left hand) was observed in a single episode. Some seizures did not progress beyond the initial phase of blank stare, head version, and unilateral motor features.

- Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; no further evolution observed in some episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic–clonic activity in other episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion; no further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with subsequent evolution to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; no further evolution observed in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity in multiple episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Asymmetric onset with right arm extension and left arm flexion, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Later, bilateral tonic-clonic movements appear. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with no further evolution in several instances; patient remains unresponsive throughout these events. In one instance, the episode evolves from leftward head version and left arm flexion into bilateral tonic stiffening followed by clonic jerks, during which the patient turns to the side while maintaining left arm flexion. Additional observations include rightward head version with right arm extension and persistent left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with loss of responsiveness. Other events begin with right arm extension and left arm flexion, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. Subtle variations include a mild leftward head turn with sustained left arm flexion and slight right arm extension without further progression.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement, manifesting initially as flexion and progressing in some episodes to extension. Subsequently, bilateral tonic stiffening occurs, followed by asynchronous clonic jerking, with greater prominence in the right arm. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases of the event.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and leftward head version, with the right arm remaining extended. Bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, progressing to asynchronous shaking and rhythmic clonic jerks. No further evolution observed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; in some instances, left arm elevation with hand grasp occurs. In cases with initial left arm flexion, bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks may subsequently develop, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Right arm extension may occur and persist throughout the event. The patient remains unresponsive in all episodes, with no further evolution observed in certain instances.

Blank stare with either subtle lip smacking or subtle leftward head version; left arm shows mild flexion or remains flexed while the right arm remains extended or shows slight extension. No further evolution or additional signs observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and subtle left arm flexion. In one instance, this evolved into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; no further evolution was observed in the other instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended or showing slight extension. Some episodes include gradual extension of the left arm while the right arm remains flexed. Lip smacking is observed in several instances, often accompanying the blank stare. Mild left arm flexion occasionally transitions into rhythmic clonic jerks of the left hand. Brief clonic jerks or bilateral tonic-clonic movements occur in a subset of episodes. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events. No further evolution or additional signs are observed in most cases. A man enters and stands beside the patient during one episode.

- Blank stare followed by subtle left arm flexion or extension, with the right arm remaining extended in most instances; one instance noted slight right arm flexion with left arm extended. - Leftward head version observed in multiple episodes, often accompanying left arm movement (flexion or extension), with one episode showing mild left arm flexion while right arm remained extended, and another noting slight right arm flexion while left arm stayed extended. - Bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks developed in several episodes following the initial blank stare and asymmetric motor signs (left arm flexion and/or head version), evolving symmetrically in some cases. - Brief rhythmic jerking isolated to the left arm occurred in one episode following leftward head version and left arm extension. - No further evolution of motor activity observed in several episodes after initial manifestations. - Patient remained unresponsive throughout all observed events, including when external interaction occurred (e.g., hand held by another person).

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and unilateral motor signs, including left arm flexion or extension and right arm extension, with progression to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or tonic–clonic movements. Right arm flexion or extension may occur initially, sometimes accompanied by a brief pause. Asynchronous shaking may begin in the left arm and spread to the right. A healthcare provider approaches at various times to adjust the nasal tube or the patient's arm. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events.

Right arm flexion and extension occur intermittently; left arm remains still. A nurse approaches and adjusts the patient's arm. Recurrent episodes of blank stare are followed by motor manifestations including left arm flexion with right arm extension, or leftward head version with left arm extension. In several instances, these evolve into bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks involving arms and legs, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout. Some events show no further evolution beyond the initial asymmetric posturing, with one episode showing the right arm remaining still initially before bilateral jerking develops. The pattern is consistent across multiple observations, indicating recurrent, stereotyped episodes of impaired awareness with motor onset in the left hemibody, variably progressing to bilateral convulsive activity.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement, manifesting as flexion or extension. Left arm flexion is consistently observed, with subsequent development of rhythmic clonic jerks or asynchronous shaking that may remain focal or spread to the right arm. Right arm may remain still or extended during initial phases, but bilateral involvement can occur. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no response to external interaction despite ongoing motor activity.

Blank stare followed by focal motor signs including unilateral arm extension (left or right) and head version (left or right), then progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient becomes unresponsive with asynchronous shaking of limbs and remains unresponsive throughout the episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, or asymmetric arm posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension; subsequently, bilateral tonic stiffening or rhythmic clonic jerks develop. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent left arm flexion and right arm extension; bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks develop, accompanied in some episodes by bilateral tonic stiffening with asynchronous shaking. One episode involved only subtle right arm flexion. During all events, the patient exhibits abrupt sitting up with automatic behaviors such as drinking from a cup before returning to lying down, and remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity involving the arms, characterized by left arm flexion or extension with contralateral right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of limbs, asynchronous shaking, and upper body movements. Patient becomes unresponsive, sits up abruptly at times, and displays bilateral hand fumbling and rubbing; otherwise lies supine with arms extended during episodes. Unresponsiveness persists throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by sudden upward gaze and leftward head version with left arm flexion; right arm extends initially, then bilateral tonic stiffening ensues. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, becoming asynchronous with predominance on the left side. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm ensue, progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening with asynchronous clonic jerks predominantly on the left side. The patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and initial left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—accompanied by subsequent rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. In one instance, the seizure progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by lateral head version and asymmetric arm movements: leftward head version with left arm flexion and right arm extension in some episodes, and rightward head version with right arm extension in others. This is followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks that intensify, during which the patient becomes unresponsive. In one instance, the patient sits up abruptly and covers the face with both hands following the initial blank stare.

Blank stare, often accompanied by subtle lip smacking or head version (leftward more commonly than rightward), is consistently observed. This is followed in many instances by left arm flexion, sometimes with right arm extension or mild right arm flexion, and occasionally isolated left leg movements. In several episodes, the initial focal features evolve into rhythmic clonic jerks involving the left arm, which may then progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Some episodes remain focal without further evolution, showing only sustained or mild limb posturing without progression. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with no signs of recovery or awareness during the observed periods.

- Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; left arm gradually extends while right arm remains flexed. No further evolution observed in multiple instances. - Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version with mild left arm flexion. Right arm remains flexed or extended in some cases. No further evolution observed. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with right arm remaining extended in several instances. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in multiple observations; patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue in repeated episodes, including both rhythmic clonic jerks and full tonic–clonic activity. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with one instance noting the mouth opening widely as a tube is inserted. - Asynchronous shaking of both arms noted during bilateral tonic–clonic movements in one episode. - Rightward head version with right arm extension observed in two instances, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic activity; patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; patient then exhibits bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Patient becomes unresponsive, arms flail asynchronously.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm ensue. Right arm remains extended throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm extension and right arm flexion; patient then exhibits bilateral rhythmic jerking. Patient becomes unresponsive as movements continue.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; patient then exhibits bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Patient becomes unresponsive during the seizure.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient then sits up abruptly. Left arm remains flexed while right arm extends. Patient grabs a green object with the left hand.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient then exhibits bilateral tonic stiffening. Asynchronous shaking ensues, with left arm showing more pronounced movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, then rightward head version and right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic activity ensues. Patient covers face with both hands and remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; later, rhythmic clonic jerks appear bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; a woman enters holding a cup. The patient then exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. The woman interacts briefly before exiting.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version; patient adjusts posture and covers face with left hand. Left arm flexes while right arm remains still, and patient becomes unresponsive.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient then adds condiment to food. No further signs of seizure activity observed.
Rightward head version with right arm extension; left arm remains flexed. Later, bilateral tonic–clonic movements appear. Patient unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and leftward head version, with subsequent bilateral tonic–clonic movements exhibiting asynchronous shaking. Additional features include rhythmic lip smacking, dropping of objects, right hand raising, right arm extension, and tonic stiffening of both arms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm initially extended in some episodes. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop in the left arm and progress to bilateral involvement; in some instances, this evolves into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The head remains midline in certain events, and right arm flexion with left arm extension is observed in one episode. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; then bilateral tonic–clonic activity ensues. Patient's arms exhibit rhythmic jerking.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and either left arm flexion or extension; subsequent bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension or leftward head version with left arm flexion, then bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks developing bilaterally; patient remains unresponsive throughout.
