Blank stare followed by asymmetric motor manifestations: one event features leftward head version with left arm extension and right arm flexion, while another shows left arm flexion and right arm extension; both evolve to bilateral asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerking. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version, left arm extension, and right arm flexion or extension; bilateral tonic stiffening ensues, with development of rhythmic or asynchronous clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes open during the episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended; bilateral tonic stiffening ensues. Rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks develop, spreading from the left side. Patient appears unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with leftward head version in one episode; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in all episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion with right arm extension; bilateral tonic–clonic movements ensue, characterized by asynchronous shaking of both arms. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event, with eyes remaining open.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion with the right arm remaining extended; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric limb involvement with left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension or rigidity, progressing to bilateral rhythmic clonic or tonic–clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.
Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor activity involving the left arm (flexion or extension) and right arm (extension or maintained extension), with subsequent development of bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by asynchronous or rhythmic clonic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases of the event.
Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with the right arm remaining extended; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

- Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient then lifts right hand towards face. No further evolution observed. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion; patient's legs exhibit rhythmic movements. No head version or lip smacking observed. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Patient's legs move asynchronously, with left leg extension and right leg flexion. - Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension; patient then exhibits rhythmic clonic jerks of the left arm. Asynchronous shaking spreads bilaterally. - Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic jerks develop bilaterally. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking; patient then brings hands together briefly before resuming initial posture. No clear head version or tonic-clonic activity observed. - Blank stare followed by subtle lip smacking. Left arm flexes while right arm remains extended. No further evolution observed.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion. In one instance, rhythmic clonic jerks appeared bilaterally; in other instances, no further evolution or additional signs were observed. Patient remained unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Subsequent evolution includes bilateral tonic–clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks involving the left arm or bilaterally.

Blank stare observed, followed in some instances by leftward head version and left arm flexion. In one episode, rhythmic leg movements occurred bilaterally and the right hand adjusted glasses before moving to the abdomen; the patient remained unresponsive during this event. No further evolution or additional signs of seizure activity were noted in the other occurrences.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, with subsequent development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In some instances, left arm flexion is accompanied by right arm extension prior to the onset of bilateral clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with no further evolution or additional signs observed in certain events.

Blank stare followed by head version and unilateral arm involvement—either rightward head version with right arm extension or leftward head version with left arm flexion—preceding evolution into bilateral tonic stiffening and subsequent clonic jerks. The patient becomes unresponsive as the seizure progresses and remains so throughout the event.

Blank stare followed by variable motor manifestations including subtle lip smacking, rightward or leftward head version, unilateral limb extension or flexion (right arm extension with still left arm; left arm flexion), evolving to bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks or generalized tonic–clonic activity. No significant limb movements observed in some episodes. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by motor manifestations including right arm extension with left arm remaining still, left arm flexion progressing to bilateral arm extension, sudden leftward head version with left arm extension, and right arm flexion with left arm extension. Bilateral tonic stiffening occurs, evolving into asynchronous clonic jerks. Rhythmic clonic jerking is observed with head maintained in midline. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, with eyes closed during events.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, often accompanied by left arm extension or right arm extension; bilateral tonic stiffening or bilateral tonic-clonic movements ensue in most episodes. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally, often asynchronous and predominantly affecting the left side. In one instance, the patient sits upright abruptly after initial movements, with subsequent right arm clonic jerks while maintaining left arm extension and later leaning back. Another episode involves interaction with a child approaching with a cup, during which bilateral tonic-clonic movements occur, ending with the patient adjusting the cup. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the majority of episodes.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm involvement—either flexion or extension—preceding bilateral tonic stiffening and evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, predominantly left-sided clonic jerks. Patient becomes unresponsive as seizure activity intensifies, with rhythmic shaking spreading from the left side to both limbs.
Right leg lifts and crosses over left leg; right hand moves to face. Left arm flexes while right arm extends; rhythmic jerks follow. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with subsequent bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks in some instances; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Blank stare followed by subtle rightward head version and right arm extension, with or without later development of bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive throughout in cases where motor evolution continues. Blank stare followed by left arm flexion alone, with asynchronous shaking evolving bilaterally in some cases; in one instance, patient adjusts clothing afterward with no further motor evolution. Right arm extension followed by left arm flexion resulting in fencer posture, with ensuing asynchronous shaking; patient remains unresponsive throughout. Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, with subsequent lifting and extension of the left leg in one instance; no further evolution observed. Blank stare followed by left arm extension and right arm flexion, with patient spreading arms wide; later, left arm returns to chest while right arm remains extended—no loss of responsiveness documented in this sequence.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension; rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally. The legs exhibit asynchronous shaking in some episodes, with isolated instances of left leg rhythmic jerking while the right leg remains still. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all events.

Blank stare followed by subtle leftward head version and left arm flexion; right arm remains extended initially. Subsequently, bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks and brief rhythmic hand movements progress to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no further evolution observed in some instances.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion, observed in multiple episodes, sometimes accompanied by rhythmic clonic jerks of the left hand or left hand rubbing the thigh rhythmically. In several instances, this evolved into bilateral tonic stiffening with leg extension and crossing, followed by generalized tonic–clonic movements. Right arm extension preceded left arm flexion in one episode. Throughout all events, the patient remained unresponsive, with some seizures remaining focal without further evolution.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and asymmetric motor signs, including left arm extension and right arm flexion or right arm remaining extended. Subsequent bilateral tonic stiffening progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms. The patient becomes unresponsive during the event, with generalized tonic–clonic movements observed throughout the seizure.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension, with concurrent left leg elevation and right hand raising, adopting a fencer posture. Leftward head version may also occur. Rhythmic clonic jerks develop bilaterally in some instances. Patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes.

Blank stare followed by left arm flexion and leftward head version; patient adjusts posture. Left hand shows fumbling movements and gestures with finger movements, reaching out to grab a phone and later moving toward the face with fingers touching the mouth area, while the right hand holds the phone steadily. Both arms exhibit subtle clonic jerks later in the episode. No further evolution of seizure activity observed, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm extension, accompanied by right arm extension or maintained extension of the right arm; subsequent bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks develop. The patient becomes unresponsive, lies supine with flaccid arms during the episode, and may open the mouth widely. Oxygen mask is applied post-seizure; patient remains unresponsive throughout the event and in the immediate recovery period.
